description: Marks the topic of the sentence. 은 attaches after a consonant-ending syllable, 는 after a vowel-ending syllable. It sets the conversational topic and can imply contrast with other items. Different from 이/가 (subject marker): 은/는 marks what the sentence is ABOUT, while 이/가 introduces new information or marks the grammatical subject.

Marks the grammatical subject of a sentence. 이 attaches after consonants, 가 after vowels. Used for new information, identification, and ability/possibility sentences. It emphasizes the subject itself, often answering 'who' or 'what'.
Marks the direct object of a verb. 을 attaches after consonants, 를 after vowels. Required with transitive verbs (eat, drink, study, etc.).

Marks location (where something exists or is placed) or direction (destination of movement). Used with 있다/없다 for existence, and with motion verbs for destination. Not used for actions happening at a place (use 에서 for that).
Marks the location where an action takes place. Unlike 에 (static location), 에서 is used when activities happen at a place. Also used to mean 'from' with motion verbs (origin point).
Marks possession or affiliation, similar to 's in English. N의 N = N's N. In casual speech, often dropped when context is clear.
Connects nouns ('and') or marks accompaniment ('with'). 과 after consonants, 와 after vowels. More formal than 하고 or (이)랑.
Connects nouns ('and') or marks accompaniment ('with'). Casual equivalent of 과/와. Used in spoken Korean.
The standard polite speech level (해요체) for present tense. 아요 follows verb stems ending in ㅏ or ㅗ, 어요 follows all others. 하다 verbs become 해요. Used in everyday polite conversation.
The formal polite speech level (합쇼체). Used in formal settings, announcements, presentations, and writing. ㅂ니다 follows vowel-final stems, 습니다 follows consonant-final stems.
Past tense ending. 았어요 for stems with ㅏ/ㅗ vowel, 었어요 for all others. 하다 → 했어요. Can also express a completed state that continues to the present.
Expresses the speaker's intention or a conjecture about the future. Also used in polite expressions: 감사하겠습니다 (I will be grateful). Attaches to the verb stem before the speech level ending.
Expresses the speaker's desire to do something. Attaches directly to the verb stem (no vowel harmony needed). To express someone else's desire, use 고 싶어하다.

Expresses ability or possibility. (으)ㄹ attaches to the verb stem (으 inserted after consonant-final stems, ㄹ added after vowel-final stems or ㄹ-final stems drop the original ㄹ). 수 있다 = can, 수 없다 = cannot.

Connects two actions sequentially ('and then') or causally ('because/so'). Key rule: the tense marker goes on the FINAL verb only, not on 아/어서. The two clauses must share the same subject for the sequential reading.

Connects two verbs/adjectives or clauses. Can mean 'and' for listing actions or properties, or 'while' for simultaneous actions. Unlike 아/어서, 고 does NOT restrict shared subjects and allows tense on either clause.

Expresses a condition: 'if/when [condition], [result]'. (으)면 attaches to action/descriptive verb stems. 으 is inserted after consonant-final stems. For ㄹ-irregular verbs, the ㄹ is dropped. Very common in everyday Korean.
Turns a verb into a modifier for a noun. (으)ㄴ = past action, 는 = present ongoing action, (으)ㄹ = future/prospective action. One of the most productive structures in Korean, replacing relative clauses.
example_pattern_use: 어제 먹은 음식, 지금 먹는 음식, 내일 먹을 음식

description: The polite form of 이다 (to be). 이에요 follows noun-final consonants, 예요 follows noun-final vowels. Used for identification: 'A is B'. Negative: 이/가 아니에요.

name_en: Negation: does not / is not (long form)
description: Long-form negation. The verb/adjective stem + 지 않다. More formal feeling than the short negation (안 + V). 않다 itself conjugates for tense and politeness.
Short-form negation. 안 precedes the verb/adjective. Natural in spoken Korean. Cannot be used with 하다 verbs directly — use N을/를 안 하다 or N 안 하다 instead of N안 하다.

Polite imperative or honorific declarative. For commands: 'Please do V'. Also used to describe the respected subject's actions (he/she does...). (으)세요 attaches to the verb stem — 으 inserted after consonant-final stems.

Expresses the speaker's intention, promise, or commitment to do something. Only used in first-person statements. Different from 겠어요 (which can express conjecture); 을게요 is more of a direct commitment to the listener.

name_en: Going to / will (future intention or guess)
description: Expresses future plans, intentions, or predictions about others. More versatile than 을게요. Can be used in first, second, and third person. When discussing your own plans, implies a current intention.
example_pattern_use: 내일 갈 거예요. / 비가 올 거예요.

description: Expresses an ongoing action (like -ing in English). Also used for durative states resulting from a completed action (e.g., 입고 있다 = 'is wearing' from putting on clothes). The auxiliary 있다 conjugates for tense.

name_en: Try doing V / have done V
description: Expresses trying an action or having tried/experienced something. 먹어 보다 = try eating / have eaten (experiential). Past tense 아/어 봤다 = tried doing, have experienced. Future: 아/어 볼게요 = I'll try doing.

Expresses doing an action for the benefit of someone else. The beneficiary is marked by 에게/한테. In requests, 주세요 makes polite commands: 봐 주세요 (please look/take care). 드리다 is the humble form (for superiors).
Used as a suggestion ('Shall we V?') or to express the speaker's conjecture ('I wonder if...'). With first-person plural implies invitation; with third person or weather contexts implies uncertainty.
Connects two contrasting clauses. The subject of both clauses can differ. Tense is usually on the second clause. More neutral than 그런데; 지만 is a single-clause connector while 그런데 is sentence-initial.
Expresses a reason from the speaker's perspective, often for commands, requests, or suggestions. Unlike 아/어서, 으니까 CAN be followed by imperatives and proposals. Tense can appear before 으니까.
Expresses concession: 'even if X, Y still holds'. The consequence in the second clause is unexpected given the condition in the first. Very common in spoken Korean.
Expresses an intention or plan to do an action. Often used to state something one is about to do. Cannot be used with adjectives.
Expresses the purpose of going or coming somewhere. The action verb attaches to -(으)러, followed by a motion verb like 가다, 오다, or 다니다.
Expresses the reason or cause for the following clause. Unlike -아/어서, this CAN be followed by imperative sentences (-(으)세요) and propositive sentences (-(으)ㅂ시다).
Expresses making a decision, promise, or resolution to do something. Frequently used in the past tense (기로 했다) to describe a decision already made.
Used with nouns to mean 'or' (choosing between options) or to express surprise that a quantity is larger than expected ('as many as', 'no less than').
Means 'only' or 'nothing but'. Must be followed by a negative form like 안, 못, 없다, 모르다. Implies that the amount or option is less than expected.
Means 'only' or 'just'. Often replaces subject/object markers. Unlike 밖에, it can be used with affirmative sentences.
Expresses inability to do something due to lack of skill, or external circumstances making it impossible. '못 V' is the short form of '-지 못하다'.
Expresses past experience (having done or not done something). Often combined with -아/어 보다 to emphasize the experience: -아/어 본 적이 있다.
Expresses that an action in the second clause happens during the time the action in the first clause is occurring. Noun form is N + 동안.
example_pattern_use: 내가 청소하는 동안 넌 설거지를 해.

description: Provides background information for the following clause, or shows contrast. The most versatile conjunction in spoken Korean. -은데 for adjectives, -는데 for verbs.
Expresses reason or cause when the predicate is a noun (이다). -이라서 after a consonant, -라서 after a vowel.
Expresses the time when an action or state occurs. Noun form is N + 때.
Expresses the starting point and ending point of a time period or sequential range.
Expresses a physical starting point and ending point (locations).
Expresses that two actions are performed at the same time by the same subject.
Used to describe an impression or guess based on someone's or something's outward appearance. Used only with adjectives.

name_en: Since I will / Since I assume...
description: Expresses the speaker's strong intention (1st person) or supposition about a situation (3rd person) as the reason for making a suggestion or request in the following clause.
Expresses a clear reason or cause. A bit more formal than -아/어서. Cannot be followed by imperatives or propositives. Noun form: N + 때문에.

name_en: Know how to / Don't know how to
description: Expresses knowing or not knowing the method/how to do something. Also used to say someone 'thought / didn't think' a certain situation would happen (-을 줄 알았다).
Expresses a possibility that something might happen or might be true, though the speaker isn't entirely sure.
Expresses that there is no other option or possibility except for the one mentioned. Also used to mean a situation is expected/natural.
example_pattern_use: 비가 와서 택시를 탈 수밖에 없었어요.

description: Provides a reason or background explanation that the listener is assumed not to know. Used mainly in spoken Korean at the end of a sentence.
Used when an unexpected or suddenly occurring event leads to a negative or unintended outcome. The trailing clause must be in the past tense.

name_en: While one is at it / Doing A and B together
description: Expresses an intention to do two or more actions simultaneously. Often the speaker mentions one purpose but hints there's another.

name_en: For the sake of / In order to
description: Expresses intention or doing an action 'for the sake of' something. Formal sounding. Noun form: N을/를 위해(서).
Expresses prohibition or restriction. Opposite of -아/어도 되다 (may/allowed to).
Expresses permission or asks for permission. 'Is it okay if I...?'

name_en: Ended up doing / Came to be
description: Expresses a change in state or situation due to external circumstances, not purely one's own will.
Indicates a change of state. 'To become + adjective'. Shows a progression from one state to another.
Expresses that in addition to the first fact, another fact exists. Noun form: N 뿐만 아니라.
example_pattern_use: 그는 똑똑할 뿐만 아니라 성격도 좋아요.

description: Used with nouns to mean 'about' or 'regarding'. N에 대한 N is used to modify another noun.
Used to remind the listener of a fact they already know, or to assert something the speaker assumes the listener agrees with.
Expresses that a certain action or event almost happened but didn't (usually a bad event or narrow escape). Always used in the past tense.
Expresses doing one action instead of another, or doing something in return or compensation for something else. Noun form: N 대신에.
example_pattern_use: 제가 밥을 사는 대신에 커피를 사세요.
